report:
## Your Health Assessment

Hi Jessica, thanks for taking the time to complete this. Your answers paint a clear picture with some important connections worth exploring. Here's what stands out.

### A Hormonal Pattern Rooted in Estrogen

Your combination of painful cramps, irregular cycles, mood swings, bloating, food cravings, anxiety, and insomnia before your period all point in a consistent direction: estrogen that is elevated relative to progesterone. This single imbalance can explain a remarkably wide range of your PMS symptoms.

Estrogen stimulates the body's stress systems, promotes water retention, increases gut sensitivity (hello, premenstrual bloating), and drives histamine release from immune cells, which fuels irritability and sleep disruption. It also promotes the kind of inflammatory signaling, driven by [prostaglandins, that directly causes menstrual pain](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35059756/).

What's important to understand is that estrogen dominance is rarely the *starting point*. It's usually the result of deeper patterns, and your other answers help reveal what those might be.

### Your Eating Pattern Is Fueling a Stress Cycle

Skipping breakfast and running on coffee until a big lunch is one of the most common yet overlooked drivers of hormonal disruption. Research shows that [habitual breakfast skipping is associated with prolonged elevation of cortisol](https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=3459&context=usdaarsfacpub), independent of perceived stress levels.

Here's the cascade that creates:

```
Skip breakfast
  → Blood sugar drops
    → Cortisol + adrenaline rise to compensate
      → You feel "fine" (running on stress hormones)
        → Big lunch finally arrives
          → Stress hormones drop → energy crashes
            → Cycle repeats at dinner
```

This pattern directly connects to several things you reported: your moderate energy (5/10), your goal of more consistent energy, and critically, your hormonal symptoms. Chronic cortisol elevation diverts the raw materials your body needs to make progesterone toward making more cortisol instead. Less progesterone means estrogen goes unopposed, and your PMS symptoms intensify.

The food cravings you experience before your period? That's your body's attempt to lower stress hormones through food. It's not a lack of willpower; it's biology.

### Diet Quality and the Inflammation Connection

Fast food, takeout, and frozen meals are among the highest sources of seed oils (soybean, canola, sunflower) and food additives. These polyunsaturated fats break down into inflammatory compounds that directly worsen menstrual pain, impair energy production, and disrupt gut function over time.

You noted no current digestive issues, which is great. But your goals of improved digestion and mental clarity suggest things aren't running optimally. The gut plays a central role in [estrogen metabolism through what's called the estrobolome](https://digbihealth.com/blogs/science-talk/the-gut-hormone-connection-how-your-microbiome-impacts-estrogen-levels), a collection of bacteria that determine how much estrogen your body recirculates versus eliminates. A diet high in processed ingredients can quietly shift this balance long before obvious digestive symptoms appear.

### The Connecting Thread: Energy

Your 5/10 energy, sleep difficulties around your period, brain fog, and the afternoon crashes that likely follow your big lunches all point to energy metabolism running below its potential. When your cells can't produce energy efficiently, everything downstream suffers: hormone production, gut function, mental clarity, and sleep quality.

```
Meal skipping + high seed oil intake + nutrient-poor diet
        ↓
  Impaired energy metabolism
     ↓              ↓              ↓
  Low progesterone   Poor sleep    Brain fog
     ↓
  Estrogen dominance
     ↓
  PMS, painful periods, irregular cycles
```

The encouraging thing is that these patterns are deeply interconnected, which means that addressing the shared root causes can improve multiple symptoms at once.

---

This assessment highlights patterns from a brief quiz, so there's naturally more to the picture. But the connections between your eating patterns, your hormonal symptoms, and your energy goals are strong and worth exploring further. There's a clear path forward here, Jessica.
